title: Tutorial
text:
Tutorial
This section will explain how to install the Python package.
The code block below is executable and will run when the site is rendered. The output appears inline with the page.

# Example Python code that will execute during Quarto render
import datetime

print("Hello from Quarto Python execution!")
print("Rendered at:", datetime.datetime.now())

Hello from Quarto Python execution!
Rendered at: 2026-04-16 18:30:21.263418


Your own tutorial can include any Python code supported by the environment, including data loading, plotting, or model evaluation.

title: Home
text:
ML Research Analysis
This website documents out Stat 386 Final Projects
Information about the purpose and findings of the project can be found under the Report tab.
Information about the python package and how to install can be found under the Tutorial tab.
Other information about the python package and the dataset can be found under the Documentation tab.
A streamlit app to explore the data can be found under the Streamlit App tab.
Click on Github to see the github repository with the python package.

title: Report
text:
Hook (1–2 sentences):
AI research has exploded in the past few years—but where is it actually going?


Track how major AI/LLM topics evolved from 2019–2026



Which AI topics grew the fastest from 2019 to 2026? Which topics had the highest publication volume each year? Which topics had the highest citation impact? How concentrated or diverse was AI research across topics over time? Did publication volume in selected topics increase after landmark papers?






We searched through several different database options for aquiring data about research papers. After exploring options such as OpenAlex and arXiv, we finally settled on SemanticScholar because their API easily provided sufficient data to analyze.



We used the following search terms when curating our dataset.

llm
CoT
reasoning
chain of thought
rag
grounding
external memory
multimodal
image generation
rlhf
lora
qlora
quantization
efficient fine-tuning
long context and efficient
sparse attention
flash attention
hallucination
efficient inference




The resulting dataset had ___ distinct entries. Columns included the following: - title - authors - year - citations - abstract - paperId - influential_citations









(Keep to 3–4 max)
GPT-3 (2020) → scaling + few-shot RAG (2020) → retrieval-based models InstructGPT (2022) → alignment Chain-of-Thought (2022) → reasoning
Goal:
See if these triggered growth in related topics




(This is your main section—very scannable)


Total papers increased from [X] → [Y] Growth accelerated around [year]
Key takeaway:
[1 sentence insight]



#1: [Topic] → +[X%] #2: [Topic] #3: [Topic]
Observation:
“Growth is concentrated in…” ### Most Popular Topics by Volume 2019: [Topic] 2022: [Topic] 2026: [Topic]
Trend:
“We see a shift from ___ → ___” ### Highest Impact Topics (Citations) [Topic] has highest avg citations [Topic] has high volume but lower impact
Interesting insight:
“Popular ≠ impactful because…” ### How Diverse Is AI Research? Early years: concentrated in [few topics] Later years: more spread out / more specialized
Interpretation:
“The field is becoming more [specialized/diverse]” ### Did Landmark Papers Change Trends?
Example 1: GPT-3 (2020)
Before: [trend] After: [trend] “Clear increase in LLM-related work”
Example 2: Chain-of-Thought (2022)
spike in reasoning papers
(Only include strong effects—skip weak ones)






AI research is shifting toward [X] Some topics grow fast but don’t dominate volume Breakthrough papers can trigger rapid follow-on work



Topic classification is imperfect arXiv bias (not all papers included) Citations lag behind recent work



AI research is not just growing—it’s shifting direction Recent years show a move toward: [example: LLMs, reasoning, efficiency]
Closing line (keep it punchy):
“Understanding these trends helps us see not just where AI is—but where it’s going next.”
